title: [perf] ft-api: reuse FFT stage twiddles for 1-D fft
description: Profile-backed target after ready queue drained and claimed perf beads avoided. Baseline: rch Criterion on ts2, cargo bench -p ft-api --bench ops_bench -- fft_1d/262144pt --warm-up-time 1 --measurement-time 5 --sample-size 10, time [18.839 ms 19.095 ms 19.339 ms]. Current tensor_fft calls dft_inplace_1d, which allocates a stage twiddle Vec per FFT stage; the existing dft_inplace_1d_with_stage_twiddles path can consume one contiguous stage-twiddle table. One lever: for power-of-two fft_len in tensor_fft, build fft_stage_twiddles once and pass it to dft_inplace_1d_with_stage_twiddles; keep non-power-of-two fallback and all n/padding/truncation behavior unchanged. Isomorphism: bit-reversal order, stage order, k order, twiddle formula, butterfly arithmetic, output order, dtype/shape/errors, and RNG unchanged; golden sha256 required. Keep only if rebench Score >=2.0.

Baseline fft_1d/262144pt on ts2 [18.839 ms 19.095 ms 19.339 ms]; after [18.261 ms 18.632 ms 18.967 ms], p50 1.0249x faster. title: porting: add tensor_nanvar + tensor_nanstd for torch parity
description:
Companion to tensor_nansum/nanmean (8q4o). Add NaN-aware variance and standard deviation:
  - nanvar(x, correction): variance over non-NaN elements; correction=0 → biased (divide by count), correction=1 → Bessel (divide by count-1, default in torch).
  - nanstd(x, correction) = sqrt(nanvar(x, correction)).
Compose through tensor_isnan + tensor_where (zero out NaN in both x and the diff term) + nanmean + sub + mul + sum + div.
close_reason: Added tensor_nanvar(input, correction) and tensor_nanstd(input, correction) in ft-api as the variance/std companion to nansum/nanmean (8q4o). Composes through tensor_isnan + tensor_where (zeros NaN in both x and the diff term) + nanmean + sub + mul + sum + div. correction=0 → biased divisor; correction=1 → Bessel-corrected (torch default); correction > count_non_nan → 0 divisor → NaN result. 6 unit tests cover biased/unbiased correctness against [1, NaN, 3, NaN, 5] (mean=3, sum_sq=8 → biased var=8/3, unbiased=4, std=2), all-NaN edge case, single-non-NaN-with-Bessel edge case, and analytic gradient regression (∂var/∂a = 0.5(a-c) for the 2-non-NaN case). ft-api now at 1315 lib tests.

title: RaptorQ sidecar generation accepts unbounded repair symbol counts
description: Reviewer finding in crates/ft-serialize/src/lib.rs and crates/ft-conformance/src/bin/run_raptorq_durability_pipeline.rs. generate_raptorq_sidecar(payload, repair_symbols) accepts an arbitrary usize, computes repair_count = repair_symbols.max(...).saturating_add(8), then calls asupersync SystematicEncoder::emit_repair(repair_count). asupersync 0.3.1 emit_repair preallocates Vec::with_capacity(count) and uses u32::try_from(i).expect("repair count exceeds u32") / checked_add(...).expect(...) inside the loop. The public CLI run_raptorq_durability_pipeline parses --repair-symbols as usize and only rejects zero, so a large value can trigger huge allocation/OOM or panic instead of a controlled SerializeError/String error. Repro by inspection: run_raptorq_durability_pipeline --repair-symbols 4294967296 reaches generate_raptorq_sidecar with a count greater than u32::MAX; do not run this on shared hosts because it may allocate enormous memory. Fix: validate/cap repair_symbols before calling emit_repair (for example a documented small max such as 255 or a function-local MAX_REPAIR_SYMBOLS), return SerializeError::IncompatiblePayload for excessive values, clamp/validate CLI args, and add regression tests for usize::MAX and u32::MAX+1 inputs.
close_reason: Added MAX_RAPTORQ_REPAIR_SYMBOLS = 1024 (user-input cap) and an internal RAPTORQ_REPAIR_COUNT_HARD_CAP = 65536 to bound the computed repair_count after k_prime padding + headroom. generate_raptorq_sidecar fails closed with SerializeError::IncompatiblePayload before any allocation, so usize::MAX / u32::MAX+1 / pathological k_prime padding values can no longer trigger asupersync's emit_repair OOM allocation or u32::try_from panic. CLI run_raptorq_durability_pipeline mirrors the input cap for a user-facing diagnostic. Added raptorq_sidecar_rejects_repair_symbols_above_max regression test covering MAX+1, u32::MAX+1, and usize::MAX.

title: pdist p=2: matmul-identity Gram + upper-triangle gather (same 50x as cdist fb6s)
description: tensor_pdist (pairwise distances WITHIN one [N,M] tensor, returns N*(N-1)/2 upper-triangle) for finite p>0 builds gathered left/right pair tensors then sub+abs+pow+sum_dim+pow — materialises a [N*(N-1)/2, M] difference tensor. Same fix as cdist fb6s (commit 574001a2): for p==2 compute the full [N,N] distance matrix via the matmul identity d^2=||x||^2+||x||^2-2*X*X^T (X@X^T Gram is one GEMM), clamp_min(0)+sqrt, then gather the i<j upper-triangle entries (flat indices i*N+j) via autograd-aware tensor_index_select on the flattened matrix. Expect ~50x for large N (cf cdist 256x256x128 = 62x). KEEP the existing path for p != 2. Validation: match direct pdist to ~1e-12 (2D), grad parity for distinct rows, golden digest; reuse cdist_bench pattern. ft-api, uncontended.
close_reason: Shipped commit fd25e65c: pdist p=2 via matmul identity (full Gram + upper-triangle gather, gather-squared-then-sqrt to avoid diagonal 0/0). Same-binary A/B: 256x128 64ms->2.3ms (28x), 512x64 132ms->7.1ms (19x). Autograd-aware, grad parity for distinct rows, duplicate rows -> exact 0, golden digest pinned, all 1854 ft-api tests pass. Pushed main+master.

title: [parity-gap] torch.special functions: xlogy, xlog1py, zeta, multigammaln, erfcx
description:
PyTorch torch.special module has several functions missing from FrankenTorch:

- torch.special.xlogy(x, y): x*log(y), with 0*log(0)=0
- torch.special.xlog1py(x, y): x*log1p(y), with 0*log1p(-1)=0
- torch.special.zeta(x, q): Hurwitz zeta function
- torch.special.multigammaln(x, p): log of multivariate gamma function
- torch.special.erfcx(x): scaled complementary error function exp(x^2)*erfc(x)

These are used in probability distributions and statistical computing.

Ref: https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/special.html
close_reason: done

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Update: xlogy, xlog1py, multigammaln already exist. Only missing: zeta (Hurwitz zeta) and erfcx (scaled erfc). Implementing those now.

title: [perf][no-gaps] ft-autograd: save_for_backward by-reference (avoid .to_vec() input copies in custom-op grad)
close_reason: Completed: f64 conv2d custom autograd now borrows saved inputs; same-worker conv2d/grad_hw/64 median 223.02 ms -> 177.43 ms (1.26x), behavior proven by golden and finite-diff tests.

comments.author: RusticKite
comments.text: STRUCTURAL lever identified from 3 overhead-bound rejections (the dominant cost here is alloc/tape, not compute). Custom autograd ops (tensor_apply_function: conv/sdpa/layer_norm/linear grad, prelu, smooth_l1, ...) call ctx.save_for_backward(values.to_vec(), shape) — a FULL COPY of each saved input, every forward. The TensorTape is append-only (inputs are never freed until truncate), so backward could fetch the input values from the tape NodeId instead of holding a copy. PLAN: add save_for_backward_ref(node_id) storing the TensorNodeId; give the backward closure tape access (or pre-resolve refs to slices at backward entry); migrate the HOT large-input ops (conv2d/conv3d grad, sdpa grad, layer/group/batch-norm grad, linear grad). Eliminates O(input-size) copies per grad op -> real training-memory + speed win. Surface: FunctionCtx + backward signature + ~N call sites -> dedicated multi-session turn. ft-autograd (non-contended). Caveat: only safe while the saved node isn't mutated in-place before backward (custom ops save inputs, which aren't in-place-mutated) — verify per op.

title: [perf] ft-serialize: slab-oriented native save byte emission
description:
Profile-backed follow-up after frankentorch-ouig, frankentorch-j125, and frankentorch-9w0q rejected smaller serializer micro-levers. Evidence: native_state_dict/save_single_f32_1m committed chunked baseline on ts1 was [892.40 us 902.04 us 913.28 us], per-value writer candidate regressed to [988.99 us 1.0017 ms 1.0145 ms]; native_state_dict/save_single_f64_1m baseline was [1.4166 ms 1.4231 ms 1.4298 ms], f64 chunk buffer candidate regressed to [1.4680 ms 1.4766 ms 1.4874 ms]; native_state_dict/decode_many_small_f64_1024x4 key-validation candidate regressed same-worker ts1 from [312.72 us 317.59 us 323.96 us] to [323.43 us 327.40 us 331.30 us]. The repeated rejection streak means stop per-value/key-parser tuning and replace the primitive.

Target primitive: slab-oriented typed byte emission for native save paths. Design a safe-Rust writer that emits exact native state_dict byte streams in larger reusable slabs for contiguous f32/f64 tensors, avoiding per-value writer calls and avoiding temporary per-key byte vectors where possible. Do not link C BLAS/MKL/XLA or introduce unsafe.

Required workflow: fresh crate-scoped RCH Criterion baseline first for native_state_dict/save_single_f32_1m and save_single_f64_1m on one worker; one lever only; preserve BTreeMap ordering, key bytes, header/layout, dtype/shape/value byte order, duplicate-key behavior, floating-point bit patterns, RNG/tie-breaking; prove exact golden SHA with ft_serialize_f32_save_bulk_pass26 and ft_serialize_f64_save_bulk_frankentorch-kgs4-37; run focused ft-serialize tests/check/clippy/fmt via RCH where applicable; same-worker rebench; keep only if Score>=2.0 and a real speedup.
close_reason: Completed f32 SWAR native save emitter: same-worker ts1 save_single_f32_1m 918.07 us -> 357.15 us median (2.57x faster, Score 9.0); f32 golden SHA unchanged; RCH focused test/check/clippy, fmt, diff check, sha passed. f64 remains next serializer target after reprofile.

title: Add ParameterList and ParameterDict containers to ft-nn

title: AlphaDropout in ft-nn severs autograd; SELU networks broken
description:
AlphaDropout at ft-nn/src/lib.rs:2146 implements its forward by extracting input values, applying the alpha-dropout transform in plain f64, and rebuilding a requires_grad=false leaf. PyTorch's torch.nn.AlphaDropout IS differentiable. Severs autograd through every AlphaDropout layer.

AlphaDropout is paired with SELU activations to maintain mean/variance through dropout — used in self-normalizing networks (Klambauer et al. 2017). Models using SELU + AlphaDropout couldn't actually train.

Math: y = a * (mask * x + (1 - mask) * sat) + a * p * sat
where mask is the random keep-or-drop mask, sat is the saturation value, a is a precomputed scale.

Composable through autograd primitives: mask leaf (non-grad) + tensor_mul (autograd) for the input branch, constant offsets via full + tensor_add for the rest.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed AlphaDropout forward through autograd-aware primitives: random mask leaf (non-grad) + tensor_mul against input + tensor_sub for inverse mask + scaled drop branch + affine offset. The transform y = a * (mask * x + (1-mask) * sat) + a*p*sat is preserved exactly but the autograd tape now flows through input. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

title: [perf] ft-kernel-cpu: mixed-precision refinement does NOT generalize to inv (high-RHS) — REJECTED
close_reason: REJECTED via same-worker A/B (one binary, inv_direct_f64 vs inv_mixed): inv_direct_512x512 [111.6 115.9 121.0]ms vs inv_mixed_512x512 [1110 1118 1126]ms = ~10x REGRESSION; 256x256 65.7ms vs 80.1ms. ROOT CAUSE: inv = solve A·X=I with n RHS (full identity). gj8t's lu_solve_mixed_refine wins for FEW rhs (rhs=32: f32 factor ~3x dominates, residual cheap) but iterative refinement RE-SOLVES per iteration + computes residual A@X = O(n^3) for rhs=n, done ~3x. At rhs=n the refinement cost SCALES WITH RHS and dwarfs the f32-factor savings. Even with a GEMM-based residual (vs the naive scalar residual_for_solve_f64) the 2 extra full n-rhs f32 solves + 3 GEMM residuals only break even (~117ms ≈ 115ms direct). LESSON: mixed-precision iterative refinement is RHS-BOUNDED — it helps low-RHS solves (gj8t 3.25x), NOT high-RHS inv/pinv. Do NOT apply it to inv. Isomorphism held (max_diff<1e-9, reconstructs I) — purely a perf rejection. No code/dead-code shipped.

title: ft-kernel-cpu: route matrix_exp matmuls through optimized dgemm
description: matrix_exp_contiguous_f64 (scaling-and-squaring Taylor) did its ~12+s n-by-n matmuls via a naive triple-loop (mat_mul_nn_generic) instead of the cache-blocked, multi-threaded gemm::dgemm. title: tensor_scatter_reduce amax/amin autograd via argmax-routing masks + scatter_add
description:
After uzca added mean autograd, only prod/amax/amin still fail loud. amax and amin can be implemented via argmax-routing:

1. Compute argmax_per_position in plain f64 (non-diff)
2. mask_input[i] = 1 if input[i] is the max (won), else 0
3. mask_src[j] = 1 if src[j] is the max at position index[j], else 0
4. result = scatter_add(mul(mask_input, input), dim, index, mul(mask_src, src))

For each output position, exactly one of input[i] or one src[j]→i 'wins'; the other terms are zeroed by the masks. Backward correctly routes gradient to the winning element via mul backward. Same pattern works for amin (find argmin instead).

prod still needs special handling (divide-back is unstable at zero); defer.

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_scatter_reduce (line 16249).

title: [parity-gap] torch.vstack, torch.hstack, torch.dstack
description:
Missing stack convenience functions:

- torch.vstack(tensors): Stack tensors vertically (along axis 0)
- torch.hstack(tensors): Stack tensors horizontally (along axis 1)
- torch.dstack(tensors): Stack tensors depthwise (along axis 2)

These are convenience wrappers around torch.cat/torch.stack.
- vstack = torch.cat along dim 0 (after ensuring at least 1D)
- hstack = torch.cat along dim 1 (after ensuring at least 1D)
- dstack = torch.cat along dim 2 (after ensuring at least 3D)

Ref: https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/torch.html
close_reason: Already implemented: tensor_vstack, tensor_hstack, tensor_dstack exist

title: pad_sequence severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
pad_sequence in ft-nn (line ~13596) extracts tensor_values from each input sequence, builds a flat output buffer with placeholder padding values, then rebuilds via session.tensor_variable(output, out_shape, false). This silently severs autograd through pad_sequence — gradients can't flow back to any input sequence. Breaks training for any RNN/LSTM/GRU pipeline that uses torch.nn.utils.rnn.pad_sequence on variable-length data.

Fix: each sequence can be padded to max_len via tensor_pad (autograd-aware), then stacked along the new batch dimension. The padding value is configurable; if non-zero, compose: padded = tensor_pad(seq, [...], 0.0) + (1 - mask) * padding_value where mask is the non-grad tensor marking the original positions. For padding_value=0.0 (the common case), tensor_pad alone suffices.

Affects: crates/ft-nn/src/lib.rs pad_sequence (line 13596 region).

title: tensor_cartesian_prod severs autograd; per-tensor scatter backward
description:
tensor_cartesian_prod at lib.rs:4065 takes 1-D tensors and emits all combinations as a 2-D output. Each output cell is a copy of one input element via a deterministic odometer mapping. PyTorch's torch.cartesian_prod IS differentiable.

Backward: for each input tensor k with N_k entries,
   grad_input_k[i] = sum over rows where indices[k] == i of grad_output[row, k]

Each input element appears in (total_rows / N_k) rows, so each contribution is sum of that many grad entries. Wrap in tensor_apply_function with this scatter-per-input backward.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Wrapped tensor_cartesian_prod in tensor_apply_function with per-tensor scatter backward. Pre-computed (row, k) → input_index_in_tensor_k mapping is captured by both forward (gather) and backward (scatter) closures so the routing is deterministic and free of per-call recomputation. Backward returns Vec<Option<Vec<f64>>> with one entry per input tensor. Added cartesian_prod_propagates_gradient_to_each_input regression test (a=[1,2], b=[3,4] → grad_a=[2,2], grad_b=[2,2] under sum loss). ft-api lib tests: 1167.

title: functional_dropout severs autograd; mask is supposed to flow gradient through input
description:
functional_dropout at lib.rs:5199 generates random mask values, multiplies in plain f64, and rebuilds a requires_grad=false leaf — silently severing autograd through dropout. Every transformer / convnet training that uses dropout would have gradients of input dropped to zero by the dropout layer's masking. PyTorch's torch.nn.functional.dropout is differentiable: the mask is sampled per-forward (non-differentiable random) but the multiplication input * mask * scale carries gradient through input.

Fix: replace the manual values+leaf assembly with composition through autograd-aware tensor_mul:
   mask_vals = 0 where dropped, scale=1/(1-p) where surviving (random per-element)
   mask = tensor_variable(mask_vals, shape, false)   # non-grad mask is correct
   output = tensor_mul(input, mask)                  # autograd-aware

Same observable forward behavior, but the autograd tape is preserved for the input.

Critical severity — this affects every dropout-using training run. Sister of the recent loss-severance cluster (ilwr / 4liu / tr2a / x9cr). Surfaced during /reality-check pass.
notes:
Fixed in this commit. Replaced functional_dropout's manual mask-and-scale body with composition through tensor_mul against a non-grad random mask. The mask values are 0 (dropped) or scale = 1/(1-p) (kept), generated per-element via the RNG; multiplication preserves the autograd tape so input gradients flow as expected. Same observable forward behavior, but training paths through dropout layers now actually train.

Critical-severity fix: every dropout-using transformer / convnet training was previously getting zero gradient downstream of any dropout layer, making training a no-op for the affected sub-network.

Added functional_dropout_propagates_gradient_through_input regression test (n=100, p=0.5 → grad entries are 0 or 1/(1-p) = 2 element-wise; sanity check that at least 10 survive). ft-api lib tests: 1155.

title: Add distributed training support (DDP/FSDP equivalents)
description:
FrankenTorch currently has no distributed-process orchestration, gradient synchronization, sharded parameter management, collective communication surface, or optimizer/state partitioning comparable to PyTorch DDP/FSDP.

Scope for eventual closure:
1. Define distributed process-group / collective abstractions
2. Add replicated gradient synchronization for DDP-style training
3. Add parameter/state sharding for FSDP-style execution
4. Preserve deterministic replay and audit evidence across multi-rank execution
5. Add conformance scenarios for cross-rank gradient equivalence and failure handling

This bead exists so FEATURE_PARITY.md can explicitly mark distributed training as deferred rather than implying it is already covered by the current CPU eager-mode slice.
close_reason: wontfix: Out of scope for CPU-first Rust port. Distributed training requires multi-process orchestration, collective communication backends, and network coordination — beyond single-machine deterministic execution.

title: Fix HingeEmbeddingLoss autograd via tensor_where + relu [child of cq0b]
description:
HingeEmbeddingLoss::forward_hinge at lib.rs:11003 computes elementwise:
   loss_i = x_i        if y_i > 0
          = max(0, margin - x_i)  otherwise
Composable: y_pos_mask = (y > 0), then mask * x + (1 - mask) * relu(margin - x). Or tensor_where over the same condition.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed HingeEmbeddingLoss through tensor_relu + mask-based blend (mask is non-grad target-derived; pos_branch = mask*input; neg_branch = (1-mask)*relu(margin-input); loss = mean(pos+neg)). All ops autograd-aware. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

title: tensor_nonzero on 0-d scalar always returns shape [0, 0]
description:
FrankenTorchSession::tensor_nonzero() at crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs:10260 silently returns shape [0, 0] for any 0-d (scalar) input — including non-zero scalars where PyTorch returns shape [1, 0].

Root cause at line 10309:
    let num_nonzero = indices.len().checked_div(ndim).unwrap_or(0);
When ndim == 0 (scalar), the inner stride loop runs 0 times so 'indices' stays empty for any value, and 0.checked_div(0).unwrap_or(0) = 0 — losing the 'is non-zero' signal entirely.

PyTorch parity:
- torch.nonzero(torch.tensor(5.0)) -> shape [1, 0]
- torch.nonzero(torch.tensor(0.0)) -> shape [0, 0]
- torch.nonzero(torch.tensor(float('nan'))) -> shape [1, 0]  (NaN treated as nonzero)

Fix: track non-zero count explicitly instead of inferring it from indices.len() / ndim, and also clean up the related tensor_nonzero_as_tuple which currently returns an empty Vec for any scalar (matches PyTorch '() tuple' but worth documenting).
close_reason: tensor_nonzero now tracks num_nonzero explicitly so 0-d scalars produce the correct PyTorch-equivalent shape: non-zero scalar -> [1, 0], zero scalar -> [0, 0], NaN scalar -> [1, 0] (NaN treated as nonzero). Previously indices.len().checked_div(0).unwrap_or(0) collapsed all scalars to [0, 0]. 3 new scalar tests added; all 1082 ft-api tests pass; clippy + ubs clean.

title: ft-api: add torch.special.airy_ai (table-free, 3-regime)
description: torch.special.airy_ai(x) was missing — the standout absent torch.special function. Added table-free Ai(x): Maclaurin (|x|<=6, via f''=xf/g''=xg recurrences) + decaying asymptotic (x>6) + oscillatory asymptotic (x<-6), with u_k=u_{k-1}(6k-5)(6k-3)(6k-1)/((2k-1)*216*k). Forward-only (torch registers no backward). Verified vs scipy across all 3 regimes (Ai(0),Ai(1),Ai(-1),Ai(5),Ai(10),Ai(-10)) + cross-branch continuity. frankentorch-parity.

Verified vs scipy across all regimes incl. negative oscillatory phase. Pushed main+master. The u_k recurrence + phase were correct first try.

title: ft-autograd: enable double-backward (create_graph) for Trace and unary rounding/step ops
description: backward_create_graph fail-loud errored for Trace, Floor, Ceil, Round, Sign, Trunc, Frac, so any second-order autograd (Hessians, gradient penalties) through a graph merely containing one of these ops crashed. Added create_graph arms: rounding/step ops (Floor/Ceil/Round/Sign/Trunc) route a constant zero gradient (f''=0 a.e.); Frac routes incoming_id straight through (d/dx=1 passthrough); Trace expands the scalar incoming gradient to [rows,cols] via cg_expand then gates it by a constant diagonal mask via cg_mul (grad_out*I), so double-backward routes the diagonal contribution upstream. Tests: create_graph_rounding_ops_zero_gradient, create_graph_frac_passthrough_gradient, create_graph_trace_second_derivative_routes_through_diagonal (L=trace(X.*X), d2/dX2=2 on diagonal).
close_reason: Implemented create_graph arms for Trace + Floor/Ceil/Round/Sign/Trunc/Frac; 419 ft-autograd tests pass (+3), clippy clean.

description: Scaled Exponential Linear Unit: selu(x) = scale * (max(0,x) + min(0, alpha*(exp(x)-1))). See https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.functional.selu.html
close_reason: done

title: porting: add tensor_pow_tensor for torch.pow(x, exponent_tensor) parity
description:
Element-wise power with a TENSOR exponent (rather than scalar — that's tensor_pow). Compose via exp(exponent * log(input)). Autograd-aware on both inputs:
  d/dinput = result * exponent / input
  d/dexponent = result * log(input)
Well-defined for input > 0; for input ≤ 0 the formula produces NaN/inf (matches torch's behavior for non-integer exponents on negative bases — torch's signed-integer-exponent path is a separate slow case).
close_reason: Added tensor_pow_tensor in ft-api: element-wise input^exponent with both operands as same-shape tensors. Composes via exp(exponent * log(input)). Autograd-aware on both: ∂y/∂x = y*e/x and ∂y/∂e = y*log(x), both from chain rule on the exp/mul/log composition. Negative bases produce NaN (matches torch.pow on non-integer exponents over negative bases — the signed-integer exponent slow path is intentionally not replicated). 4 unit tests cover known values, gradient regression at (x=4, e=3) verifying ∂/∂x=48 and ∂/∂e=64*ln(4), shape-mismatch rejection, and negative-base NaN behavior. ft-api now at 1319 lib tests.

title: weight_norm_reconstruct severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
weight_norm_reconstruct in ft-nn (line 13718) extracts tensor_values from g and v, computes w = g * v / ||v|| in plain f64, and rebuilds via session.tensor_variable(w_values, v_shape, false). This silently severs autograd: gradients can't flow back to either g (magnitude) or v (direction), breaking training for any model using nn.utils.weight_norm (WaveNet, certain GANs, normalizing-flow variants).

Fix: compose via autograd primitives. Math:
  w[i, ..., dim_idx, ..., j] = g[dim_idx] * v[i, ..., dim_idx, ..., j] / ||v||[dim_idx]
where ||v||[dim_idx] = sqrt(sum over non-dim axes of v^2).

Implementation: permute v so dim is first, reshape to [dim_size, others], compute v*v, sum_dim along axis 1, sqrt, clamp_min(eps); broadcast g and norm to v's permuted shape; divide; multiply; reshape and permute back to v_shape.

Affects: crates/ft-nn/src/lib.rs weight_norm_reconstruct (line 13718)

title: [perf] ft-api: parallelize orthogonal-polynomial special functions (serial vs torch)
description: GAP: 12 orthogonal-polynomial special functions (chebyshev t/u/v/w + shifted t/u/v/w, hermite h/he, laguerre l, legendre p) evaluated each element serially via vals.iter().map(|&x| FN(n,x)); these are O(n) degree-n recurrences per element (most compute-bound special fns). torch evaluates vectorized/parallel. LEVER: added a shared par_map_f64(vals, f) helper (gates on PARALLEL_ELEMENTWISE_MIN=8192 -> par_iter) and routed all 12 sites through it. ISOMORPHISM: pure per-element maps, bit-identical (orthogonal_polynomials_parallel_match_serial_bit_exact + existing value tests). BENCH (RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1 vs all cores, legendre_p degree 64, 256K elems): 110.5->27.1ms (4.08x). Helper now reusable for the Bessel family follow-up.
close_reason: Parallelized 12 orthogonal-polynomial special fns via shared par_map_f64 helper (gated 8192); bit-exact (orthogonal_polynomials_parallel_match_serial_bit_exact); legendre_p64 256K 110.5->27.1ms 4.08x. 1740 ft-api tests pass, my hunks clippy-clean.

title: porting: add tensor_nansum and tensor_nanmean for torch parity
description: Add tensor_nansum and tensor_nanmean ops mirroring torch.nansum / torch.nanmean. Both treat NaN as missing data (zero contribution). Compose through autograd-aware tensor_isnan + tensor_where so gradients flow only to non-NaN positions. nanmean uses the count of non-NaN elements as the divisor. Useful for ML workflows with masked / missing data.
close_reason: Added tensor_nansum and tensor_nanmean ops mirroring torch.nansum / torch.nanmean. nansum: composes through tensor_isnan + tensor_where(zeros) + tensor_sum so NaN positions contribute 0 to the sum and 0 to the gradient (where routes upstream grad to the all-zero constant which has no requires_grad). nanmean: same as nansum then divides by (numel - nan_count). ±inf passes through unchanged (matches PyTorch — only NaN is masked). All-NaN edge case returns NaN via 0/0 divisor. 6 unit tests added: nansum_treats_nan_as_zero, nansum_passes_through_inf, nansum_propagates_gradient_to_non_nan_positions, nanmean_divides_by_non_nan_count, nanmean_all_nan_returns_nan, nanmean_propagates_gradient_scaled_by_one_over_count. ft-api now at 1223 lib tests.

It copies input and overwrites the offset-diagonal with src, so grad splits: grad_input = grad_out with diagonal zeroed, grad_src[i] = grad_out at diagonal position i. 2-input apply_function; bit-identical forward. frankentorch-avfl-diagscatter.

title: [perf] ft-serialize: SWAR-pack half native save bytes
description: Profile-backed pass 6 target after ready perf queue was empty and active perf claims were on ft-kernel-cpu/ft-optim. Fresh RCH Criterion profile on ts2 at 2026-06-05: native_state_dict/save_single_f16_1m [1.6249 ms 1.6291 ms 1.6332 ms], native_state_dict/save_single_bf16_1m [1.6617 ms 1.6666 ms 1.6732 ms], slower than f32/f64 save lanes. Hotspot hypothesis: write_native_f16_values/write_native_bf16_values emit two bytes per value via per-value extend_from_slice; a safe SWAR-style emitter can pack four u16 payload bits into one u64 little-endian lane before extending the staging buffer. One lever only: replace half/bfloat16 per-value byte appends with shared four-at-a-time u16-bit packing. Proof obligations: BTreeMap key order unchanged; dtype tags and shape headers unchanged; Float16/BFloat16 payload bits and little-endian byte order exactly unchanged; no FP arithmetic, RNG, or tie-breaking changes; golden native f16/bf16 save SHA unchanged.
close_reason: Completed: SWAR-packed half native save bytes. Same-worker ts1 Criterion medians improved f16 save_single_1m 930.52 us -> 181.00 us and bf16 940.35 us -> 182.88 us (both 5.14x faster, Score 9.0). Golden f16/bf16 native save SHA unchanged; RCH focused test/check/clippy and fmt/diff checks passed.

title: [perf] ft-data: branchless three-weight sampler threshold
description: Profile-backed fallback after br ready had no ready perf beads and active claimed surfaces were ft-kernel-cpu/GEMM, ft-optim, and ft-nn MHA. RCH ts1 Criterion baseline for sampler/weighted_three_positive_3x1m: [4.5017 ms 4.5622 ms 4.6206 ms]. RCH ts1 profile-time completed for the same row; the source target is WeightedRandomSampler::indices three-weight fast path, which loops 1,000,000 samples through an if/else threshold chain while the four-weight path already uses a branchless threshold helper. One lever: replace only the three-weight conditional chain with a threshold_index_3 helper using usize::from(u > threshold) sums. Preserve validation/error order, cumulative threshold arithmetic, RNG call count/order, <= boundary semantics, output ordering, tie behavior, and golden sha256. title: Add metamorphic invariant tests for softmax/log_softmax/transpose/inverse equivalences
description:
Existing fuzz harness in ft-conformance covers 4 ops (abs, add, sum, matmul) with shape/finiteness assertions. There are no metamorphic equivalence tests — checking that two algebraically-equivalent paths produce the same output within ULP tolerance.

Catch-cases this would lock down:
1. exp(log_softmax(x)) ≈ softmax(x)   — composition equivalence
2. log(softmax(x)) ≈ log_softmax(x)   — direct vs explicit
3. softmax(x).sum() ≈ 1.0             — probability invariant
4. softmax(x + c) ≈ softmax(x)        — translation invariance
5. transpose(transpose(x)) ≡ x        — involution
6. matmul(I, A) ≈ A                   — identity invariance
7. matmul(A, A.inv()) ≈ I             — inverse invariance (well-conditioned A)
8. (a + b).sum() ≈ a.sum() + b.sum()  — sum linearity

Each can be a proptest with random valid inputs. The log_softmax precision bug fixed under frankentorch-ebrb would have been caught by (1) and (2) — that's the kind of bug these target.

Filed during /testing-metamorphic round of the multi-pass cycle.
notes:
Fixed in this commit. Added 4 metamorphic property tests inside the existing #[cfg(feature='fuzz')] proptest! block in ft-conformance:
  - fuzz_metamorphic_log_softmax_equals_log_of_softmax (the case that would have caught frankentorch-ebrb at random inputs)
  - fuzz_metamorphic_softmax_sums_to_one (probability-distribution invariant)
  - fuzz_metamorphic_softmax_translation_invariance (softmax(x + c) == softmax(x))
  - fuzz_metamorphic_double_transpose_is_identity (bit-exact involution)
ULP-tolerant comparators with absolute-floor fallbacks. ft-conformance lib tests with --features fuzz: 135 passing.

title: [bug][perf] ft-api: f32 no-grad conv2d errored (f64 panel vs f32 weight) — add fused conv2d_forward_f32
description: functional_conv2d no-grad path: f64 -> conv2d_forward_f64, but EVERY non-f64 dtype fell through to a serial im2col gather that built an f64 panel (let mut panel = vec![0.0; panel_len]) and tensor_matmul'd it against the f32 weight -> dtype mismatch -> Err. So f32 INFERENCE conv2d was BROKEN (returned Err, panicked under .unwrap()). Proven: reverting the fix makes ops_bench conv2d/f32_hw panic at the tensor_conv2d unwrap. FIX: added ft_kernel_cpu::conv2d_forward_f32 + conv2d_im2col_f32 (f32 mirrors of the f64 fused kernel via sgemm_bt) and wired the f32 no-grad branch to them. Bit-exact to the composed op-graph path (functional_conv2d_f32_no_grad_fast_path_matches_composed_path, to_bits). Fused f32 conv timings: hw32 13.5ms, hw64 39.4ms, hw128 133.9ms (parallel im2col + sgemm_bt; the f64 equivalent was 2.85x vs its serial predecessor). Grad f32 path unchanged.
close_reason: SHIPPED 75a4e62d: f32 no-grad conv2d was returning Err (serial im2col built an f64 panel matmul'd against the f32 weight -> dtype mismatch). Added fused conv2d_forward_f32 + conv2d_im2col_f32 (sgemm_bt mirrors of the f64 kernel) + wired the f32 no-grad branch. Bit-exact vs composed op-graph (functional_conv2d_f32_no_grad_fast_path_matches_composed_path). f32 conv now works AND is fast (parallel im2col + sgemm_bt): hw32 13.5/hw64 39.4/hw128 133.9ms.

title: Add TorchScript/JIT and torch.compile-style graph execution
description:
FrankenTorch currently executes an eager CPU-first tensor/autograd stack. It does not expose graph capture, TorchScript serialization/execution, ahead-of-time lowering, or `torch.compile`-style graph optimization/runtime selection.

Scope for eventual closure:
1. Define graph capture / IR boundaries for eager programs
2. Add TorchScript-like serialization or a compatible alternative representation
3. Add compile-time graph lowering / optimization pipeline for `torch.compile`-style workflows
4. Preserve observable tensor semantics and DAC evidence under compiled execution
5. Add conformance scenarios comparing eager vs compiled outputs and gradients

This bead exists so FEATURE_PARITY.md can explicitly mark JIT / `torch.compile` work as deferred instead of silently omitting a major PyTorch subsystem.
close_reason: wontfix: Out of scope for CPU-first Rust port. JIT/torch.compile requires graph IR, compilation pipeline, and runtime code generation — FrankenTorch targets eager-mode execution with deterministic autograd.

title: tensor_bincount severs autograd through weights tensor
description:
ft-api tensor_bincount (line 3651) extracts both input and weight tensor_values, builds the bin sums in plain f64, and rebuilds via tensor_variable(_, _, false). The input (bin indices) is integer-valued and intrinsically non-differentiable, but PyTorch's bincount IS differentiable through the weights — silently severing here breaks training for any use of weighted bincount in custom losses (focal loss in detection, class re-weighting, etc.).

Fix: compose via tensor_scatter_add. Build a zeros tensor of shape [n_bins], use input (cast to f64 index tensor) as scatter index, and weights as src. The unweighted case still returns counts (use ones tensor as src).

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs tensor_bincount (line 3651).

title: Parallelize grid_sample (2-pass coord resolve + plane gather) — 6x
description: grid_sample_f64 ran a single-threaded n,h,w,c loop -> a 1M-element grid_sample (spatial transformers / optical flow / deformable conv) took ~17ms on one core. Restructured into 2 passes: PASS 1 resolves the source coordinate (ix,iy) once per (n,h,w) [unnormalize + padding mode], PASS 2 distributes the [out_h*out_w] (n,c) output planes across the rayon pool and gathers (nearest/bilinear). Compute-bound (the gather dominates), so it parallelizes near-linearly. Same-worker RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1-vs-default A/B: 16.9ms -> 2.80ms = 6.0x. Bit-exact (identical float ops, deterministic; 11 grid_sample tests + the independent-reference bit-exact guard pass). A prior row-parallel attempt got only ~1.3x; the (n,c)-plane structure + coords-resolved-once is the lever. 1860 ft-api tests pass.

title: ft-api: fused single-pass im2col panel for conv2d (kill permute copy)
description:
Largest vs-upstream gap in the suite. ops_bench conv2d/hw/32 (batch=4 in_ch=64 out_ch=64 k=3x3 stride1 pad1) measured 220.58 ms p50 in committed pass frankentorch-61ds vs PyTorch 2.12 conv2d_32x32 p50 3.58 ms (benchmarks/pytorch_baseline.json) -> ~61x gap. conv2d_64x64 anchor 28.16 ms, conv2d_128x128 73.18 ms.

Root cause (functional_conv2d, crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs ~15664): im2col is built as a 4-stage tensor pipeline unfold(dim2)->unfold(dim3)->permute([0,2,3,1,4,5])->reshape, then transpose(weight)+matmul. For conv2d/hw/32 the unfolded_hw tensor is [4,64,32,32,3,3] = 2.36M f64 = ~19 MB, and tensor_permute MATERIALIZES a second ~19 MB copy purely to move the in_ch/kernel axes next to each other. Each stage also allocates a fresh autograd node (>=6 nodes/call) even though the bench tensors are requires_grad=false. The matmul itself ([4096,576]x[576,64], ~150 MFLOP) is a small fraction of the cost; the gap is memory-bound im2col + permute copy + node churn.

Lever (ONE): replace the unfold->unfold->permute->reshape->transpose chain with a single fused im2col gather that writes the [batch*out_h*out_w, in_ch*kh*kw] panel directly in patch-major, (in_ch,kh,kw)-minor order in one pass over the padded input, then one matmul against the flattened weight. Eliminates the ~19 MB permute copy and 3-4 intermediate autograd materializations. Alien-graveyard anchor: nested-data-parallel flattening / packed-panel GEMM packing (im2col is the convolution analogue of GEMM panel packing).

Constraints / proof obligations: output bit-exact to current path (same NCHW layout, same patch gather order, same accumulation order in matmul so floating-point identical); must preserve backward (scatter) semantics for the requires_grad path OR keep the composed path for grad and fast-path only no-grad — must be proven against existing conv2d gradient tests bit-exact; dtype/shape diagnostics unchanged; no RNG.

Acceptance: rch crate-scoped fresh baseline first (conv2d/hw/32,64,128 on one worker); ONE lever; deterministic bit-exact golden sha256 vs current output AND existing conv2d backward tests; rebench same worker; keep only if Score>=2.0 and a real speedup (target >=1.5x on conv2d/hw/32, ideally toward closing the 61x gap across hw/64 and hw/128).
notes: Fresh same-worker rerun captured in artifacts/optimization/2026-06-03_ft_api_conv2d_fused_panel_frankentorch-a29q.md: clean HEAD on rch ts2 hw/32 [182.32 ms 183.45 ms 184.52 ms], hw/64 [897.21 ms 900.57 ms 903.93 ms], hw/128 [3.8836 s 4.0330 s 4.2500 s]; after fused no-grad panel hw/32 [36.719 ms 36.922 ms 37.221 ms], hw/64 [141.93 ms 143.11 ms 144.12 ms], hw/128 [610.28 ms 613.29 ms 616.27 ms]. p50 speedups 4.97x/6.29x/6.58x; Score 10.0; golden sha256 fb414ed3ce5db49ddfdd26e37e647754580f18884d9417333bc4cd0468859445.
close_reason: Completed ft-api fused no-grad im2col panel. Same-worker ts2 conv2d/hw/32 baseline [182.06 ms 182.81 ms 183.60 ms] -> [45.178 ms 45.899 ms 46.346 ms], p50 3.98x faster, Score 10.0. Proof: functional_conv2d_no_grad_fast_path_matches_composed_path_bit_exact passed on ts2; cargo check -p ft-api --all-targets passed with pre-existing warnings; golden sha256 fb414ed3ce5db49ddfdd26e37e647754580f18884d9417333bc4cd0468859445.

title: [perf] ft-api: parallelize lgamma/digamma elementwise eval (serial vs torch)
description: GAP: ft-api had NO rayon — special functions (lgamma=libm::lgamma, digamma=recurrence+asymptotic series) evaluated each element in a serial vals.iter().map(approx) inside tensor_apply_function; compute-bound, used in Gamma/Beta/Dirichlet log-probs. torch vectorizes/parallelizes these. LEVER: added rayon to ft-api; gate forward+backward maps of tensor_gammaln/tensor_digamma on PARALLEL_ELEMENTWISE_MIN(8192) -> par_iter. ISOMORPHISM: pure per-element maps, bit-identical to serial (existing 14 gamma/digamma value tests pass + new proof). BENCH (RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1 vs all cores, 1M elems): lgamma 74.2->33.5ms (2.22x), digamma 88.4->34.7ms (2.55x). Speedup diluted by the serial save_for_backward memcpy.
close_reason: Parallelized lgamma/digamma elementwise eval (fwd+bwd) via rayon par_iter gated at 8192; bit-exact (lgamma_digamma_parallel_match_serial_bit_exact + 14 existing value tests); lgamma 74.2->33.5ms 2.22x, digamma 88.4->34.7ms 2.55x. 1738 ft-api tests pass. (pre-existing unrelated ft-api clippy debt elsewhere, not touched.)

title: backward_create_graph silently swallows tensor read errors as empty gradients
description:
TensorTape::backward_create_graph (crates/ft-autograd/src/lib.rs ~12933, ~12958) calls grad_node.tensor.contiguous_values_as_f64().unwrap_or_default() in two places.

If contiguous_values_as_f64() returns an error (e.g. UnsupportedLayout for a non-contiguous result, or StorageSpanOverflow for a malformed meta), unwrap_or_default() yields an empty Vec, and the leaf's gradient silently becomes [].

Risks:
- The TensorBackwardReport reports a None/empty-but-nominally-success gradient.
- persistent_grads accumulator zip-iterates over an empty 'vals', leaving the entry untouched on second-and-later backward passes — silently breaking gradient accumulation across calls.

This violates the project's non-regression rule (autograd correctness outranks kernel speed) by hiding a DenseTensorError as a missing gradient.

Fix: propagate the error via ? into AutogradError (already has From<DenseTensorError>), so a malformed gradient tensor surfaces at the backward boundary instead of being silently dropped.
close_reason: backward_create_graph now propagates DenseTensorError via ? at both gradient-extraction sites instead of unwrap_or_default-ing tensor reads to empty Vec. Malformed grad tensors surface as AutogradError::DenseTensor at the backward boundary, restoring the 'autograd correctness outranks kernel speed' invariant. All 310 ft-autograd + 1082 ft-api tests pass; clippy + ubs clean.

title: Add messages to bare unreachable!() calls (5 sites)
description:
## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found 26 `unreachable!()` calls across the codebase. 21 already have explanatory messages or surrounding comments. 5 do NOT have messages, making debugging harder if they ever fire due to future code changes.

## Bare unreachable!() sites to fix
These are the sites that lack an explanatory message string:

1. ft-dispatch/src/lib.rs — multiple bare unreachable!() in dispatch routing match arms
2. ft-autograd/src/lib.rs:2947 — bare unreachable!() in backward pass routing

The remaining 21 sites already have messages like `unreachable!("complex dtypes rejected above")` and need no changes.

## Plan
1. For each bare `unreachable!()`, add a message string explaining WHY it is unreachable, e.g.:
   `unreachable!("all dispatch key variants handled above")`
2. Do NOT change logic — only add the message parameter
3. Verify with clippy that no new warnings are introduced

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 26 unreachable!() calls across the workspace have explanatory message strings
- Zero logic changes
- Clippy clean

## Reasoning
If the "impossible" state ever becomes possible due to a new enum variant or refactor, the message tells the developer exactly which invariant was violated. This is a defense-in-depth measure aligned with the DAC correctness doctrine.
close_reason: Added explanatory messages to all remaining bare unreachable!() sites across crates and verified workspace check/clippy via rch

title: Add compile-time exhaustiveness guard for DType match arms
description:
## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found 26 unreachable!() calls. Many exist because match arms on DType handle a subset of variants and use unreachable!() for the rest after an early validation check. This pattern is fragile: if a new DType variant is added, the early check might be updated but the match arm might not, causing a panic.

## Problem
The DType enum has 9 variants (F64, F32, F16, BF16, I64, I32, Bool, Complex64, Complex128). Functions that only handle a subset (e.g., floating-point types) use an early if-guard then unreachable!() in the catch-all. A better pattern is to use a helper that converts the subset to a sub-enum, making the match exhaustive at compile time.

## Plan
1. Create typed helper enums or use existing type-level dispatch patterns:
   - FloatingDType { F64, F32, F16, BF16 } with TryFrom<DType>
   - NumericDType { F64, F32, I64, I32 } with TryFrom<DType>
2. Convert the most dangerous unreachable!() sites (those in hot paths like dispatch and backward) to use these sub-enums
3. The match becomes exhaustive and the compiler catches missing arms on enum changes
4. Less critical sites (test helpers, edge cases) can keep unreachable!() with messages

## Acceptance Criteria
- At least the 14 unreachable!() sites in ft-dispatch are converted to exhaustive matches
- New DType variants cause compile errors (not runtime panics) in dispatch code
- All existing tests pass

## Dependencies
- Depends on: frankentorch-dwy (DenseTensor::storage panic fix) -- both touch DType handling
- Should be done AFTER the storage() refactor to avoid merge conflicts

## Reasoning
This is the highest-leverage defense-in-depth change. It converts 14 potential runtime panics into compile-time errors, which aligns with the DAC correctness doctrine and Rust's zero-cost abstraction philosophy.
close_reason: Replaced all 14 ft-dispatch DType/promotion unreachable!() sites with typed non-complex storage classification, added regression tests for complex rejection and BF16 fallback, and verified ft-dispatch tests plus workspace check/clippy via rch

title: Implement special_i1 and i1e backward instead of fail-loud guards
close_reason: done: special_i1 + i1e backward fully implemented in commit 3484dab — fail-loud requires_grad guards removed, replaced with analytical backward (d/dx i1 = i0 - i1/x, removable zero-limit 0.5; scaled variant for i1e). Tests i1_propagates_gradient + i1e_propagates_gradient verify against bessel_i1{,e}_derivative_scalar. Bead was never closed.

title: [perf] ft-kernel-cpu: multi-threaded row-blocked GEMM (close single-thread vs ATen/MKL gap)
description: GAP: matrixmultiply is single-threaded, so a large solo matmul/bmm/conv/linear used one core while torch's BLAS (MKL/OpenBLAS) is multi-threaded. PRIMITIVE (alien-graveyard: cache-blocked GEMM + embarrassingly-parallel row partition): split the GEMM output across M-row blocks on the rayon pool, each block an independent matrixmultiply call over a contiguous row range. ISOMORPHISM: per output element the k-accumulation order is fixed by the micro-kernel independent of row count -> bit-for-bit identical (proved by gemm_row_split_matches_single_bit_exact, f64+f32). BASELINE (RAYON_NUM_THREADS=1) vs OPTIMIZED (all cores), same binary: f64 1024^3 44.28->15.31ms (2.89x), f32 1024^3 21.84->8.04ms (2.72x), f64 512^3 5.43->2.36ms (2.30x), f32 512^3 2.69->1.16ms (2.33x). Threshold: flops>=1<<20 and m>MIN_BLOCK_ROWS so small matmuls keep the single-call path. Safe Rust (rayon), no new unsafe.
close_reason: Multi-threaded row-blocked GEMM; bit-exact (gemm_row_split_matches_single_bit_exact f64+f32); f64 1024^3 2.89x, f32 1024^3 2.72x, 512^3 ~2.3x. 370 ft-kernel-cpu tests pass, clippy clean.

title: tensor_cumulative_trapezoid severs autograd; same composition as trapezoid + cumsum
description:
tensor_cumulative_trapezoid at lib.rs:15689 is the cumulative variant of trapezoid: instead of summing all weighted-avg pairs it returns the running cumulative sum. The body extracts values, computes the cumulative sum in plain f64, and rebuilds a non-grad leaf. PyTorch's torch.cumulative_trapezoid IS differentiable.

Same composition as tensor_trapezoid (frankentorch-b4ys) but with tensor_cumsum at the end instead of tensor_sum_dim:
   y0 = narrow(y, dim, 0, n-1)
   y1 = narrow(y, dim, 1, n-1)
   avg = (y0 + y1) * 0.5
   dx = (x1 - x0) if x else 1
   result = cumsum(avg * dx, dim)
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_cumulative_trapezoid through narrow + add + mul + cumsum primitives — same recipe as tensor_trapezoid (b4ys) but with cumsum instead of sum_dim. All ops autograd-aware. Added cumulative_trapezoid_propagates_gradient_through_y regression test (y=[1,2,3,4] → cumulative=[1.5, 4.0, 7.5]; under sum loss, grad_y=[1.5, 2.5, 1.5, 0.5] computed via reverse-cumulative routing). ft-api lib tests: 1173.

title: [code-review] nan reductions reject F32 tensors through F64 where branches
description: [HIGH] crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs:6061,6079,6122,6144 build zero replacement tensors with full(..., 0.0), which creates F64 tensors. For F32 inputs, tensor_isnan returns an F32 mask and tensor_where requires condition/x/y to have matching dtypes (crates/ft-autograd/src/lib.rs:7956), so tensor_nansum, tensor_nanmean, tensor_nanvar, and tensor_nanstd reject valid F32 inputs. PyTorch nanmean/nansum accept floating input dtypes, and the recent isposinf review already fixed the same dtype-matched-constant pattern. Fix by constructing replacement/divisor constants in the input dtype and preserving F32 through tensor_where; add F32 regressions for nansum/nanmean/nanvar/nanstd.

title: tensor_aminmax severs autograd; composable through min_dim/max_dim
description:
tensor_aminmax at lib.rs:15031 computes both min and max along a dim. Output is non-grad leaves. PyTorch's torch.aminmax IS differentiable through both branches (gradient routes via argmin/argmax to the corresponding input indices).

tensor_min_dim and tensor_max_dim already exist and are autograd-aware (they delegate to the tape's max_dim / min_dim primitives). Compose tensor_aminmax through them — discarding the indices since aminmax doesn't return them.

Surfaced during /reality-check pass continuing from cummax/cummin (wkkw).
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_aminmax through tensor_min_dim + tensor_max_dim (autograd-aware via the tape's argmin/argmax-routing reductions). Discarded the index outputs since aminmax doesn't return them. Added aminmax_propagates_gradient_via_argmin_argmax_routing regression test. ft-api lib tests: 1162.

title: Add metamorphic property tests: sigmoid complement, exp/log roundtrip, abs of product
description:
Extend ft-conformance metamorphic suite with three more invariants:

1. fuzz_metamorphic_sigmoid_complement_sums_to_one — sigmoid(x) + sigmoid(-x) == 1 within ULP envelope. The two sides of the sigmoid curve sum to 1 by definition.

2. fuzz_metamorphic_exp_log_roundtrip — log(exp(x)) ≈ x for x in a safe range (-30..30) within a small ULP envelope.

3. fuzz_metamorphic_abs_product_equals_product_of_abs — abs(x * y) == abs(x) * abs(y) bit-exactly (sign-bit math).

title: porting: add tensor_heaviside for torch.heaviside parity
description: torch.heaviside(input, values) — Heaviside step function: 0 for x<0, 1 for x>0, values[i] for x==0. Compose via tensor_gt(x, 0) for the step part + tensor_eq(x, 0) for the special-value part using tensor_where. Non-differentiable in general (the step has a Dirac at 0), so fail-loud on requires_grad to avoid silently severing the tape.

Non-differentiable: fail-loud on requires_grad to avoid silent severing. 4 unit tests cover the standard cases (-1, 0, 1, 2 → 0, val, 1, 1), per-element values at zero, requires_grad guard, and shape-mismatch rejection. ft-api now at 1270 lib tests.

title: tensor_trapezoid severs autograd; composable through narrow/add/mul/sum_dim
description:
tensor_trapezoid at lib.rs:15607 computes the trapezoidal-rule integral along a dim. PyTorch's torch.trapezoid IS differentiable — it's linear in y (and bilinear-ish in x via dx pre-computation).

Composable through autograd primitives:
   y0 = narrow(y, dim, 0, n-1)
   y1 = narrow(y, dim, 1, n-1)
   avg = (y0 + y1) * 0.5
   dx = if x is provided: x1 - x0 (similar narrow). else: 1.0 broadcast.
   weighted = avg * dx
   integral = sum_dim(weighted, dim)

All ops autograd-aware so gradients flow to y (and x when provided). Surfaced during /reality-check pass.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed tensor_trapezoid through narrow + add + mul + sum_dim primitives. Math: y0 = narrow(y, dim, 0, n-1); y1 = narrow(y, dim, 1, n-1); avg = (y0 + y1) * 0.5; dx = (x1 - x0) if x else 1; integral = sum_dim(avg * dx, dim). All ops autograd-aware. Added trapezoid_propagates_gradient_through_y regression test (y=[1,2,3,4], uniform spacing → integral=7.5, grad_y=[0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5]). ft-api lib tests: 1172.

title: torch.special functions lack subprocess conformance: digamma, polygamma, logit, xlog1py, entr, multigammaln
description: All torch.special.* functions in ft-api/lib.rs are hand-rolled approximations (digamma_approx, polygamma_approx, etc.) but lack subprocess conformance against scipy.special. The existing unit tests assert single known values within 1e-6 — too loose to catch silent precision regressions. Add subprocess conformance harnesses comparing each against scipy.special.{digamma, polygamma, logit, xlog1py, entr, multigammaln}. Pattern: matches the existing libm subprocess conformance tests (torch_erf_erfc_libm_subprocess_conformance etc.) — ctypes-from-libm or scipy-via-numpy oracle, ULP-tolerant comparison.
notes: All slices added across recent commits: digamma (subprocess conformance done in earlier round), polygamma (b1012df), xlog1py (a3ea89a), entr (188aa4a), logit (284a0ab), multigammaln (this commit). Each test fail-loud against scipy.special with ULP / relative-tolerance comparators. Total ~150 inputs covered across the six harnesses.

title: ft-api: add tensor_resize_as_ (shape-match only)

title: functional_embedding severs autograd via tensor_values + non-grad rebuild
description:
ft-api functional_embedding (line 5411) extracts tensor_values from both indices and weight, looks up rows in plain f64, and rebuilds via session.tensor_variable(result, out_shape, false). This silently severs autograd through the weight tensor — breaks training for any model that uses F.embedding directly (transformers using token embeddings outside the nn.Embedding wrapper, custom layers, etc.).

The corresponding nn.Embedding module impl (Module for Embedding in ft-nn) already composes via tensor_reshape + tensor_index_select; functional_embedding should do the same.

Fix: compose via tensor_reshape(input, [n_indices]) + tensor_index_select(weight, 0, flat_indices) + tensor_reshape to [*input_shape, embedding_dim].

Affects: crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs functional_embedding (line 5411).

title: tensor_autograd_grad mishandles grad_outputs (broken VJP)
description: tensor_autograd_grad ran backward with a ones seed and then elementwise-multiplied the i-th INPUT's gradient by grad_outputs[i] — conflating the output index with the input index and applying the seed in the wrong vector space. PyTorch's grad_outputs seeds the backward per-output, yielding the vector-Jacobian product Sum_k J_k^T v_k (a contraction over the output dimension). The bug was invisible for diagonal Jacobians (where J^T v == (J^T ones) elementwise* v) but wrong for any mixing Jacobian, and it corrupted the public tensor_functional_vjp which delegates here. Fix: build a scalar surrogate s = Sum_k <output_k, v_k> with v_k a non-grad constant (defaulting to ones) and run one ordinary backward; d s/d input = Sum_k J_k^T v_k exactly, no tape changes. Added matmul-based regression (non-diagonal J, v!=ones), diagonal case, VJP test, and grad_outputs length-mismatch guard. ft-api autograd_grad/vjp/hvp/create_graph tests pass.
close_reason: Rewrote tensor_autograd_grad to build a scalar surrogate s = Sum_k <output_k, v_k> (v_k non-grad, default ones) and run one backward, giving the exact VJP Sum_k J_k^T v_k. Fixes both autograd.grad(grad_outputs=...) and functional_vjp. Added matmul non-diagonal regression + diagonal + vjp + length-mismatch tests; all ft-api autograd_grad/vjp/hvp/create_graph tests pass.

title: ft-api: ctc_loss mean reduction must divide by target length (torch parity)
description: tensor_ctc_loss reduction='mean' did a PLAIN mean of -logZ over the batch; torch divides each per-batch loss by its target length (clamped >=1) BEFORE averaging. Fixed by composing div-by-target-length + mean (autograd-aware: divisor is a non-grad constant, so each -gamma gradient is scaled by 1/(N*L_b)). 'sum'/'none' unchanged. Verified mean == mean_b(none_b/max(L_b,1)) + gradient row-sum invariant -1/(N*L_b).
close_reason: done

Added TensorTape::values_lossy_f64 / Session::tensor_values_lossy_f64 (converts F32/F16/BF16->f64 via contiguous_values_as_f64) and routed histc+mode through it. Test histc_mode_support_f32_inputs: histc(f32)==histc(f64), mode(f32) correct. ft-api 1885 + ft-autograd 435 pass. bincount f32 deferred to dh5f (needs scatter_add index dtype). Pushed main+master.

title: [parity-gap] torch.nn.functional.prelu
description: Parametric ReLU: prelu(x, weight) = max(0,x) + weight * min(0,x). Weight is learnable per-channel. See https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.functional.prelu.html
close_reason: done

title: affine_grid lacks autograd: linear in theta, easy backward [child of 3v6e]
description:
tensor_affine_grid currently has a fail-loud guard rejecting requires_grad=true theta. Math is purely linear in theta — for each (n, h, w) output cell:
    grid[n,h,w,0] = t00*x_w + t01*y_h + t02
    grid[n,h,w,1] = t10*x_w + t11*y_h + t12
where (x_w, y_h) are derived from output_size and align_corners (no theta dependence).

Backward (vector-Jacobian):
    grad_theta[n,0,0] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,0] * x_w
    grad_theta[n,0,1] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,0] * y_h
    grad_theta[n,0,2] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,0]
    grad_theta[n,1,0] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,1] * x_w
    grad_theta[n,1,1] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,1] * y_h
    grad_theta[n,1,2] = sum_{h,w} grad_grid[n,h,w,1]

Wrap in tensor_apply_function with these accumulator loops in the backward closure. No saved tensors needed beyond the output_size + align_corners (captured by the closures). Standard PyTorch parity — torch.nn.functional.affine_grid supports backward.

Child of frankentorch-3v6e.
notes: Fixed in this commit. Added an autograd path inside tensor_affine_grid that wraps the linear forward + analytical backward in tensor_apply_function for F64 theta. F32 theta with requires_grad=true continues to fail-loud (preserves dtype semantics; tensor_apply_function is f64-only). Backward accumulates grad_theta from grad_grid by reapplying the (x_w, y_h) coordinates that the forward used. Added tensor_affine_grid_propagates_gradient_through_theta regression test in ft-api covering the analytical [0, 0, 4, 0, 0, 4] gradient under sum loss. Renamed the existing fail-closed test to tensor_grid_sample_fails_closed_for_grad_inputs (grid_sample autograd is still a separate child of frankentorch-3v6e). ft-api lib tests: 1137.

title: perf: matmul 500x slower than PyTorch — needs BLAS backend
description:
FrankenTorch matmul is ~500x slower than PyTorch (single-threaded):
- 64x64: FT 5.0ms vs PT 9μs (556x)
- 128x128: FT 36ms vs PT 49μs (744x)  
- 256x256: FT 141ms vs PT 365μs (385x)

Root cause: ft-kernel-cpu uses naive tiled triple-loop matmul with SIMD dot product, not optimized BLAS.
PyTorch uses OpenBLAS/MKL with cache-aware micro-kernels, proper blocking, and prefetching.

Fix options:
1. Add optional OpenBLAS/MKL/BLIS bindings via blas-sys crate
2. Implement production-quality GEMM with L1/L2/L3 blocking
3. Use matrixmultiply or faer crate as intermediate solution

Evidence: criterion benchmarks vs pytorch_baseline.json
close_reason: Fixed via matrixmultiply integration in commit 5fac9df

title: add tensor_gumbel_softmax
description: differentiable categorical sampling with Gumbel-softmax trick and optional straight-through estimator
close_reason: done

title: [perf] ft-api einsum: contraction-path order REJECTED (permute-overhead-bound); real lever = einsum_binary transposes
close_reason: REJECTED greedy contraction-path for multi-operand einsum. It was CORRECT (16 einsum tests + 1881 ft-api pass; value-matches left-to-right <1e-9, grads flow) but a PERF REGRESSION: A/B chain 'ab,bc,cd,de->ae' [2048x8,8x2048,2048x8,8x2048]: manual-matmul-left-to-right 6.22ms vs greedy einsum 70.44ms (11x SLOWER); at 256x8 it was 485us vs 1307us. ROOT CAUSE: einsum is einsum_binary-OVERHEAD-bound, NOT contraction-order-bound. einsum_binary sets up each pairwise contraction by MATERIALIZING full-tensor transposes via the op-graph tensor_permute (to feed contiguous matmul); at scale those 32MB transposes dominate, and the greedy's index orderings + the final output permute trigger large ones, swamping the ~3x flop savings. So the contraction-PATH optimization is invisible/counterproductive under the permute overhead. CONTRAST multi_dot (i3ez, 3.7x): it composes raw tensor_matmul directly (no per-step transpose materialization), so reordering wins there. THE REAL EINSUM LEVER: eliminate einsum_binary's transpose materialization — route its matmul through a dgemm_bt-style strided read (read operands transposed WITHOUT materializing, the tool from the linear/conv fusion work) so contractions don't pay full-tensor permutes; THEN the contraction-path optimization would also pay off. Bigger change (grad implications) — dedicated turn. No code/regression shipped.

title: ft-nn LPPool1d/LPPool2d sever autograd; composable via unfold + abs + pow + sum_dim
description:
LPPool1d at lib.rs:12086 and LPPool2d compute (sum |x|^p)^(1/p) over each kernel window. Body extracts values, computes manually in plain f64, rebuilds non-grad leaf. PyTorch's torch.nn.LPPool* IS differentiable.

Composable through tensor_unfold (autograd-aware after frankentorch-hc18) + abs + pow + sum_dim + pow:
   for LPPool1d: unfold input on the length dim → [N, C, out_len, kernel_size]
   abs(unfolded), pow(norm_type), sum_dim(over kernel_size dim), pow(1/norm_type)
notes: Fixed in this commit. Composed both LPPool1d and LPPool2d through tensor_unfold + abs + pow + sum_dim + pow (all autograd-aware after the unfold fix in frankentorch-hc18). For LPPool2d, nested unfold over H then W yields a [N, C, oh, ow, kh, kw] tensor that's reduced via two sum_dim calls. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

title: Linear algebra ops lack subprocess conformance: svd, qr, cholesky, det, slogdet, inv, pinverse, solve, eigh, lstsq, matrix_norm
description: FEATURE_PARITY.md claims parity_green for these ops but none have subprocess conformance against numpy.linalg / scipy.linalg. Add diff-tests across well-conditioned and edge-case matrices (identity, scaled identity, singular, near-singular, complex eigenstructure). Pattern: matches torch_vector_norm_numpy_subprocess_conformance / torch_diff_numpy_subprocess_conformance — numpy oracle, ULP- or relative-tolerant comparison. Coverage: at least det / slogdet / inv / solve as the highest-impact subset; the others can follow incrementally.
notes: All available linalg slices added across recent commits: det (earlier round), slogdet (e8cfc36), inv (75dbb04), solve (7115a2a), cholesky (ea904bb), qr (9a86171), svd (62ac52d), pinv (6e86432), eigh (a4b2758), lstsq (this commit). matrix_norm has no separate API in ft-api (vector_norm covers the existing surface and was already locked by torch_vector_norm_numpy_subprocess_conformance). Ten subprocess harnesses against numpy.linalg, all fail-loud, with structural-invariant comparisons for the sign-ambiguous decompositions (qr/svd/eigh) and element-wise comparisons for the unique outputs (det/slogdet/inv/solve/cholesky/pinv/lstsq). One real bug surfaced: SVD doesn't complete the orthonormal basis on rank-deficient inputs — tracked under frankentorch-zs8a.

title: Add CUDA/GPU execution backend
description:
FrankenTorch currently exposes `Device::Cuda` tags and device-guard validation, but all real execution remains CPU-only. There are no CUDA kernels, no device memory model, no dispatcher backend selection beyond CPU, and no runtime integration for GPU execution.

Scope for eventual closure:
1. Add concrete GPU storage/device abstractions beyond enum tagging
2. Implement CUDA dispatch keys and backend routing
3. Port core kernels (elementwise, reductions, matmul, indexing) to GPU
4. Preserve deterministic autograd/DAC evidence across cross-device execution
5. Add conformance and benchmark coverage for CPU↔GPU parity

This bead exists so FEATURE_PARITY.md can truthfully mark CUDA/GPU as deferred rather than silently omitted.
close_reason: wontfix: Out of scope for CPU-first Rust port. CUDA requires GPU memory model, kernel porting, and device runtime — fundamentally different from deterministic CPU tensor library goals.

title: masked_scatter all-false fast path drops source zero-gradient
description: Reviewer finding in crates/ft-api/src/lib.rs. The current tensor_masked_scatter implementation returns Ok(input) immediately when n_true == 0 at lines 11682-11688. That preserves input passthrough but removes the source tensor from the backward graph entirely. The adjacent regression test masked_scatter_all_false_mask_passes_input_through_with_grad at lines 33742-33761 says source gradient should be zero, but it only asserts input_grad, so the missing source gradient is not covered. Repro by code inspection: create input requires_grad=true, all-zero mask, source requires_grad=true, call tensor_masked_scatter, sum the output, run tensor_backward, then query tensor_gradient(report, source). Because the fast path returns the original input node, compute_reachable in ft-autograd only reaches input and tensor_gradient(source) is None instead of a zero vector of source.numel(). This silently violates the deterministic autograd contract for a tracked source argument. Fix by composing the all-false case through an autograd-aware zero dependency on source, or by adding an explicit masked_scatter op/backward rule that records source and emits zero source gradients when no mask entries consume it. Add a regression assertion that all-false masks produce source_grad == vec![0.0; source_numel].
close_reason: Removed the n_true==0 fast-path return-input shortcut for non-empty source — the gather + where composition now runs unconditionally for source_numel >= 1, so source stays in the backward graph and tensor_gradient(source) returns zeros(source.shape) instead of None. Empty-source case kept as a fast path since its gradient is shape-empty regardless. Added masked_scatter_all_false_mask_with_empty_source_returns_input edge-case test and strengthened masked_scatter_all_false_mask_passes_input_through_with_grad to assert source_grad == zeros.

title: functional_normalize severs autograd; composable through abs/pow/sum_dim/div
description:
functional_normalize at lib.rs:5151 computes y = x / max(||x||_p along dim, eps) but the body extracts values, divides in plain f64, and rebuilds a requires_grad=false leaf — silently severing autograd through every L2-normalization layer. PyTorch's torch.nn.functional.normalize IS differentiable.

Compose through autograd primitives:
   abs_x   = tensor_abs(input)
   pow_x   = tensor_pow(abs_x, p)
   sum_x   = tensor_sum_dim(pow_x, dim)
   norm    = tensor_pow(sum_x, 1.0 / p)
   norm_c  = tensor_clamp_min(norm, eps)
   norm_kd = tensor_unsqueeze(norm_c, dim)
   norm_b  = tensor_expand(norm_kd, original_shape)
   y       = tensor_div(input, norm_b)

All ops are autograd-aware so gradients flow back to input. Sister of frankentorch-7yvv (dropout severance) — both are critical for training paths.
notes:
Fixed in this commit. Composed functional_normalize through abs/pow/sum_dim/clamp_min/reshape/expand/div primitives — same observable y = x / max(||x||_p along dim, eps) but with the autograd tape preserved.

The norm reduction is reshaped to a keepdim=true-style broadcast shape (input's shape with dim set to 1) so the subsequent expand + div work uniformly across 1-D and higher-rank inputs.

Added functional_normalize_propagates_gradient_through_input regression test for input=[3,4] L2-normalize on dim=0:
   y = [0.6, 0.8] under sum loss → grad = [(1 - y_i * sum_y) / ||x||_2] = [0.032, -0.024]
asserts to 1e-12.

ft-api lib tests: 1156.

title: Add end-to-end training example and integration test
description:
FrankenTorch has e2e training loop tests buried in ft-optim's test suite, but there is no standalone, documented example showing users how to train a model. This is critical for proving the system works end-to-end and for user onboarding.

Create a comprehensive integration test (in ft-conformance or a new examples/ directory) that:

1. Defines a 2-layer MLP: Linear(784, 128) -> ReLU -> Linear(128, 10)
2. Generates synthetic MNIST-like data using the existing TensorDataset/DataLoader
3. Trains for 5 epochs using Adam optimizer with learning rate scheduling (StepLR or CosineAnnealing)
4. Tracks loss per epoch and asserts it decreases monotonically (with tolerance for noise)
5. Uses no_grad context for evaluation pass
6. Saves model state_dict to file, loads it back, verifies predictions match
7. Uses BatchNorm or LayerNorm in the architecture to exercise normalization paths
8. Logs: epoch number, batch count, loss per epoch, final accuracy estimate, elapsed time

This test exercises the full stack: ft-data (DataLoader) -> ft-nn (modules) -> ft-api (forward/backward) -> ft-autograd (gradient computation) -> ft-optim (parameter update) -> ft-serialize (save/load).

If any component fails to compose correctly, this test catches it.

Tests:
- The example IS the test: it should be a #[test] fn in ft-conformance or similar
- Assert loss decreases over 5 epochs
- Assert save/load round-trip produces identical predictions
- Assert no_grad eval context doesn't corrupt gradient state
- Log detailed per-epoch metrics for debugging
close_reason: Added 5 E2E integration tests exercising the full stack: MLP regression with DataLoader/Adam/StepLR, binary classification with cross-entropy, save/load round-trip verification, no_grad eval safety, dataloader epoch shuffle. All pass.

title: Diagnose residual beads integrity-check warning

title: MaxUnpool1d/2d/3d sever autograd; scatter-then-gather backward needed
description:
MaxUnpool1d (line 6149), MaxUnpool2d (line 6237), and MaxUnpool3d (line 6337) all extract input values, scatter them via indices in plain f64, and rebuild requires_grad=false leaves. PyTorch's torch.nn.MaxUnpool* IS differentiable.

Backward: each output position came from a single input position (via the indices) — gather grad_output from those positions back to grad_input. Sister of repeat_interleave / diag_embed / unfold (gather-scatter pattern, except here forward is scatter and backward is gather, so the mapping inverts).

Each variant has the same forward/backward structure with different spatial indexing.
notes: Fixed in this commit. All three MaxUnpool variants (1d/2d/3d) now wrap their forward in tensor_apply_function with the analytical scatter-then-gather backward. The (input_pos → output_pos) indices mapping is captured by both forward and backward closures; backward gathers grad_output from the destination positions back to grad_input. ft-nn lib tests: 517.

Non-differentiable (inherits tensor_complex's requires_grad guard, consistent with fft/polar complex-autograd policy). Test cfloat_cdouble_cast_real_and_complex (real f64->C128, f32->C64, passthrough, C128->C64 downcast). No cross-crate changes.

Test cfloat_cdouble_cast_real_and_complex passes (f64->C128, f32->C64, passthrough, C128->C64 downcast). Closes a torch-parity gap (these always errored UnsupportedDType before). 1761 ft-api lib tests, clippy clean.

title: [parity] ft-api: implement linalg_cholesky NxN autograd backward (lower factor)
description: GAP: torch.linalg.cholesky is differentiable but our NxN path failed loud (only 1x1 had a backward; frankentorch-rl4g). IMPL: standard differentiable-Cholesky (Murray 2016 / PyTorch cholesky_backward) in f64 via tensor_apply_function: phi=tril_half_diag(L^T@grad_L); A_bar=L^-T@phi@L^-1 (L^-1 via forward substitution); grad_A=(A_bar+A_bar^T)/2. Lower factor, square F64 (torch default); upper/non-F64 still fail loud. Verified by finite-difference gradient check (cholesky_nxn_backward_matches_finite_difference, directional derivative with symmetric perturbation). 16 cholesky tests pass.
close_reason: Implemented linalg_cholesky NxN autograd backward (lower factor, square F64) via the PyTorch cholesky_backward formula in f64: phi=tril_half_diag(L^T grad_L); A_bar=L^-T phi L^-1 (forward-substitution L^-1); grad_A=(A_bar+A_bar^T)/2. Helper cholesky_backward_lower. FD-verified (cholesky_nxn_backward_matches_finite_difference, directional derivative). upper/non-F64 still fail loud (bounded scope). 16 cholesky tests pass, clippy clean (no new warnings). Closes a real torch-parity autograd gap.

title: [parity] ft-api: linalg_cholesky upper-factor autograd backward
description: Extends the lower cholesky backward (frankentorch-cholesky-backward-wmef) to the UPPER factor. A=U^T U with U=L^T, so feed L=U^T and grad_L=grad_U^T to cholesky_backward_lower; grad_A is symmetric so no transpose-back. Unified the requires_grad branch to handle both upper/lower (square F64). FD-verified (cholesky_upper_backward_matches_finite_difference). 17 cholesky tests pass.
close_reason: Extended linalg_cholesky autograd backward to the upper factor: A=U^T U, U=L^T -> feed L=U^T, grad_L=grad_U^T to cholesky_backward_lower (grad_A symmetric, no transpose back). Unified requires_grad branch (upper+lower, square F64). FD-verified (cholesky_upper_backward_matches_finite_difference). 17 cholesky tests pass, clippy clean.

title: [bug][parity] ft-api scalar wrappers add/sub/mul/div_scalar upcast f32->f64 (weak-scalar parity)
close_reason: Shipped 2ccb8ea0 (worktree off HEAD to avoid live ft-api peer churn). scalar_leaf_matching_dtype helper casts the scalar leaf to the input dtype (F32/F16/BF16); F64 + non-float paths bit-identical. add/sub/mul/div_scalar now preserve f32 (torch weak-scalar parity). Test f32_scalar_arithmetic_preserves_f32_dtype passes; ft-api 1874 + ft-nn 708 green. Pushed main+master.

comments.author: RusticKite
comments.text: ROOT CAUSE: Session::{add,sub,mul,div}_scalar (ft-api/src/lib.rs ~55147) build the scalar via full(vec![1], scalar, false) which makes an F64 leaf, then call the binary op. tensor_add/sub/mul/div promote f32+f64 -> f64 (test f32_mixed_promotes_to_f64 @74748), so f32_tensor.add_scalar(2.0) silently returns F64. TORCH PARITY: python scalars are WEAK (torch.float32 tensor + 2.0 -> float32). Same bug class as tensor_mul_scalar (FIXED 908ef05f). Affects F32/F16/BF16 inputs (~2x memory + wrong dtype in f32 inference). FIX: cast the scalar leaf to the input's dtype before the binary op (F64 + non-float unchanged). No existing test pins the upcast.

title: ft-api: lp_pool 1d/2d/3d autograd (Lp-norm window backward)
description: functional_lp_pool1d/2d/3d silently severed autograd. Lp-pool out=(sum|x|^p)^(1/p) over each window is smooth; d out/dx = s^(1/p-1)|x|^(p-1)sign(x). Added shared lp_pool_grad_output(input, windows, p, out_shape): forward sums in caller order (bit-identical), backward scatter-adds the per-window derivative (overlapping windows accumulate). Each fn builds per-output window index lists in the requires_grad branch. FD-verified with overlapping windows. frankentorch-avfl-lppool.

title: ft-api: zeta autograd w.r.t. q (missed silent-sever, same class as igamma)
description: tensor_zeta (Hurwitz zeta, special.zeta) silently severed autograd. torch differentiates it w.r.t. q (the 2nd arg, parameter): dzeta(s,q)/dq = -s*zeta(s+1,q); s (1st arg, exponent) is non-differentiable (matches torch derivatives.yaml). 2-input apply_function returning [None, Some(grad_q)]. Bit-identical forward, FD-verified + closed-form -2*zeta(3,2). frankentorch-2vxa.
close_reason: Shipped commit 93d528bc: zeta autograd w.r.t. q (dzeta/dq=-s*zeta(s+1,q), s non-diff per torch). 2-input apply_function [None, Some(grad_q)]. Bit-exact forward + FD + closed-form verified. Pushed main+master.

title: ft-nn loss modules sever autograd: HingeEmbeddingLoss, FocalLoss, MultiMarginLoss, others
description:
Multiple loss modules in ft-nn extract values, compute the per-element loss in plain f64, and rebuild a non-grad leaf via tensor_variable(_, _, false). The subsequent reduction (mean/sum/none) operates on this severed leaf so gradients never reach the input.

Affected (line numbers from current main):
  - HingeEmbeddingLoss::forward_hinge (lib.rs:11003)
  - FocalLoss / its forward (lib.rs:~11800, near 11810)
  - MultiMarginLoss (lib.rs:~11827)
  - Other reduction-style loss modules in the 11800-12100 range

PyTorch's torch.nn.HingeEmbeddingLoss / FocalLoss / MultiMarginLoss are all differentiable. Each loss has a closed-form composition through autograd-aware primitives.

Children: each loss gets its own fix bead. Sister of frankentorch-2med (the session-method loss umbrella that closed earlier).
notes: Children landed: HingeEmbeddingLoss (8530), MultiMarginLoss (1s4q), SoftMarginLoss (fjao), FocalLoss multi-class softmax variant (kxcz), MultiLabelMarginLoss (r38w). Each loss module now composes through autograd-aware primitives instead of severing via tensor_values + tensor_variable(false).
